paragraphs.body: In my family the way we communicate shows a blend of love and affection but in our own unique way. It shapes our identities and values in many ways. Our conversations often involve playful teasing and sharp witty comebacks. My family and friends create an environment where humor becomes a form of connection. It is because of this reason, I am who I am. Honestly, I would not have it any other way. It has taught me the importance of toughness and versatility, and how to not take things so seriously. My family and friends express how we feel while also challenging each other, revealing both our flaws and strengths. It is common for a lighthearted jab to come right after a heartfelt compliment, it is just how we love each other. This language of tension really shows a deep understanding of one another, reinforcing our bonds and teaching us that love is not always soft or sentimental, it can be complex and sarcastic. It is because of this dynamic, I have come to appreciate how my family and friend's interactions reflect our cultural background, where love and admiration often dances with intensity. This way of affection and tension has shaped my beliefs about relationships, emphasizing that vulnerability and strength can coexist, and that love can be found even in moments of derision.

To speak in my house and speak with my friends means you have to have thick skin and a witty comeback, rather quickly.

paragraphs.body: Growing up in this environment has profoundly shaped how I view relationships with people. The laughter we share with each other and the playful jabs my family and my friends take at each other, has taught me that love is not just all about soft words and gentle gestures. Love can be expressed by being real with one another or keeping it “100 percent” as me and my friends say. My family and friends have taught me to embrace the fact that we are not perfect and use humor to navigate life's challenges. For instance, I am really home sick so when I can talk to my mom on the phone, she makes me laugh by telling me how I could miss home when my little brother is acting like a “brat” and “eating everything in sight”. She uses humor and sarcasm to calm me down and make me realize that the distance between us is only temporary and not forever. Having this approach reminds me that even in challenging times, we can find moments of joy together although we are across the county.

annotations.comment: Lovely ideas. I wish they were written in your authentic voice :)

paragraphs.body: Sometimes on Sunday nights I get really lonely because all my teammates go home, and I'm stuck in my room all by myself. It is then that I facetime one of my friends Freddie from back home in California. His light-hearted jabs about how big I am or how much I ate that day, makes me not so lonely anymore and makes me realize that me going to college across the country is God's plan for building my character. Freddy’s humor not only lifts my spirits but also reinforces the idea that these challenges are opportunities for growth and connection, even from a distance.

Moreover, our way of communicating has instilled in me a deep sense of resiliency. I’ve learned that criticism doesn't diminish love because even though my mom calls us little “brats”, she will always be there for me and my family and if I need anything she will always be there with open arms. It is for this reason; I believe that in our family every jab is just a reminder that we love each other eternally. This dynamic with my family has influenced how I relate to friends and colleagues, encouraging them to value open communication and humor in all my relationships. When my friends and I spend time together, we always use humor and criticize each other.

annotations.text: Moreover, our way of communicating has instilled in me a deep sense of resiliency.
annotations.comment: Is this in your voice? This is the kind of writing that suggests to me that you're using A.I. Is this a sentence you would ever say in real life?

paragraphs.body: Keeping in contact with my best friend Isaiah while he is in Tennessee playing football has become super important to me as well. It is during these conversations that we tease each other about eating too many candy bars or him being too ugly to get a girl. Do I really think Isaiah is ugly? No, but it makes for great comic relief when we are both struggling with being homesick and missing each other. As I reflect on these experiences, I realize that this blend of love that I have, and tension has become a cornerstone of my identity and is the reason I am the person that I am today. It is a reminder that love can be powerful and complex. In a world where being open or vulnerable can feel risky or scary, my family and friends show me that true connections thrive on honesty, authenticity, and a bit of humor. They create a safe space where sharing our fears, dreams, and imperfections is not only accepted but is celebrated in a way. Their support reassures me that it is acceptable to be flawed, which inspires me to embrace my own faults. I have discovered in this setting that being open can result in significant development and toughness, supporting the notion that love, and acceptance thrive when we have the courage to be who we really are.

The distinct communication style within my family and my friends has shaped my view on the world. Humor and honesty have equipped me with the tools to go through life challenges with confidence. In addition to growing resilience, this atmosphere makes me feel like I belong, which enables me to see the beauty in our common flaws. As I move forward with life, I am inspired to create relationships that show authenticity and encourage growth. I thank my mother for teaching me to use humor in uncomfortable situations to help lighten the conversations during difficult times. It is because of her that I recognize that true connection is based on understanding and acceptance, reminding me that it is perfectly okay to embrace my flaws while finding my true strengths. I have lit the path for deeper and more meaningful connections with people. Life is about building connections with people. These connections give us purpose and meaning to our lives because without a purpose in life you are just living to live and not enjoying the gift of what it means to LIVE LIFE!!

annotations.comment: This essay is pretty much the same form and structure as the first one you submitted. I see that you've made some line edits here and there, but I don't see any real change that would suggest to me that this essay is written entirely and solely by you, in your voice. We write to get better at writing! You have such great ideas and perspectives; I want to hear about them. The product, the final essay, is not the most important thing, here. It's the process, the risk, the trying that matters in this class. If you don't take that risk yourself--if you don't think and write and try new things that might be hard for you--then how can you get better? I believe in you. I know you can do this! I'll assign this essay a passing grade because--technically--it fulfills the requirements of the prompt, but I really hope you rewrite it.
